summary: Jeff Atwood revisits his passion for arcade emulation a decade after his original builds, discovering that the Raspberry Pi has fundamentally transformed the hobby. The Pi 3's $35 price point, combined with WiFi/Bluetooth, sufficient power to emulate N64/PSX/Dreamcast, and the mature RetroPie project, makes emulation accessible to anyone. Chinese all-in-one JAMMA boards and cheap IPS LCDs have further reduced costs and complexity. What once required $1,500+ can now be accomplished for $100-500 in a weekend, making it an ideal family maker project.
key_points: The Raspberry Pi 3 at $35 can emulate virtually every significant console and arcade game created before 1997, a capability that would have seemed magical to earlier generations
Three technological shifts enable cheap emulation: Raspberry Pi maturity, Chinese JAMMA boards (~$90), and affordable IPS LCD panels (~$100)
RetroPie has evolved from complex hobbyist software to near plug-and-play simplicity since 2013
The total investment has dropped from $1,500+ to $100-500, democratizing what was once an expensive niche hobby
A bartop arcade represents the sweet spot for most builders: reasonable size, portability, good screen size, and two-player support
money_quotes: I think I spent most of my childhood – and a large part of my life as a young adult – desperately wishing I was in a video game arcade.
The ascendance of Raspberry Pi has single-handedly revolutionized the emulation scene.
At this point you have a fully functioning emulation brain for well under $100 which is capable of playing literally every significant console and arcade game created prior to 1997.
Your 1985 self is probably drunk with power.
I remember spending well over $1,500 to build my old arcade cabinets. I'm excited that it's no longer necessary to invest that much time, effort or money to successfully revisit our arcade past.
Thanks largely to the Raspberry Pi 3 and the RetroPie project, this is now a simple Maker project you can (and should!) take on in a weekend with a friend or family.
key_insight: The Raspberry Pi has democratized arcade emulation, transforming a $1,500+ expert hobby into a $100-500 weekend project accessible to anyone willing to learn.
